text:
Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers a diverse range of B.Tech programs that cater to various engineering disciplines as well as business and computing fields. Established since 1999, the Mechanical Engineering program has been a cornerstone with an intake of 120 students annually. The Computer Science & Engineering (CSE) program, initiated in 2001, boasts the largest intake at 180 students per year, reflecting its growing importance in today's digital world.

Electrical Engineering, introduced in 2004, and Civil Engineering, launched in 2010 with an intake of 60 students each, have been pivotal in shaping the technical landscape. Aeronautical Engineering, which began in 2013 with a smaller intake of 60 students, has seen steady growth and relevance. The Food Technology program, introduced in 2019, addresses emerging food industry challenges.

In recent years, Annasaheb Dange College has expanded into cutting-edge fields such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Data Science, which commenced in 2021 with an intake of 60 students. Similarly, the CSE program has diversified to include Internet of Things and Cyber Security Including Blockchain Technology, also starting in 2021 with a similar intake size.

For the academic year 2025-26, the college introduces two new programs: Robotics And Artificial Intelligence (intake of 60 students) and Bachelor of Business Administration (BBA), both debuting with an intake of 60 students. Additionally, the Bachelor of Computer Application (BCA) program will also begin in this academic year, further enriching the curriculum to meet the evolving demands of industry and academia.

metadata.label: B.Tech
programs: Computer Science & Engineering (Internet of Things and Cyber Security Including Block Chain Technology)

text: For the academic year 2025-26, Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers four M.Tech programmes, each with an intake of twelve students. The Mechanical Engineering Design programme, which began in 2025, focuses on advanced design methodologies and real-world applications, preparing students for roles that require innovative problem-solving skills. Similarly, the Civil Engineering (Computer Aided Structural Engineering) programme also commenced in 2025, equipping students with cutting-edge tools to enhance structural analysis and design processes. The Computer Science and Engineering programme, introduced in the same year, aims to bridge the gap between theory and practice by integrating software development, data science, and cybersecurity into a comprehensive curriculum. Lastly, the Electrical Power System programme, which also started its first intake in 2025, is designed to address the growing demand for professionals who can manage complex power systems, ensuring reliability and efficiency in energy distribution networks. These programmes collectively offer diverse specializations catering to various engineering disciplines, providing students with a robust foundation for their careers.

For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the tuition fees are structured differently based on the reservation category for both First Year (FY) and Direct Second Year (DSE) admissions. For students from the OPEN category, the annual tuition fee is ₹134,026 for FY and DSE admissions. Similarly, candidates belonging to EBC/EWS/OBC categories will have to pay an annual fee of ₹82,505 for both FY and DSE. The SBC/VJNT/TFWS category students are required to pay a lower annual tuition fee of ₹30,983 for both FY and DSE admissions. Lastly, SC/ST candidates are eligible for the lowest annual tuition fee at ₹15,526 for both FY and DSE admissions. This transparent fee structure ensures that all students have clear information about their financial obligations before applying to the college.

For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, students have several hostel options to choose from based on gender. For female students, there are two main choices: the New Ladies Hostel with an annual fee of ₹35,000, offering modern amenities and facilities; and Subhadra Ladies Hostel, which comes at a slightly lower cost of ₹30,000 per year. Male students have access to both new and old hostels, with the New Boys Hostel costing ₹30,000 annually, providing contemporary living spaces, while the Old Boys Hostel is more budget-friendly at ₹20,000 per year. Each hostel offers a comfortable environment for students to reside during their engineering studies, ensuring they can focus on academics and extracurricular activities without the worry of accommodation costs. Students should carefully consider their financial situation and personal preferences when selecting a hostel to ensure a smooth transition into college life.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers its students a convenient bus service named "Ashta-Palus," catering to their daily commute needs. The route begins at the college campus in Ashta and extends through several key locations including Nagthane Fata, Amnapur, Palus, Sawantpur, Kirloskarwadi, and finally reaches Palus Colony. Students are required to pay a monthly fee ranging from ₹500 for travel between Ashta and Nagthane Fata to ₹2,100 for the journey from Ashta to Palus Colony. This diverse range of costs reflects the varying distances covered along the route, ensuring that students can choose a plan that suits their specific needs and budget. The bus service is designed to provide reliable transportation, enhancing the overall commuting experience for students as they travel between home and college each day.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers a convenient college bus service named "Ashta-Miraj" to facilitate commuting students. The buses operate from Ashta to Miraj, passing through several key locations including Karandwadi/Tung/Mirajwadi, KasbeDigraj, KarnalChouk, Sangli, and finally reaching Miraj. Monthly transport costs vary based on the stop: students traveling via Karandwadi/Tung/Mirajwadi or KasbeDigraj pay ₹1,000 and ₹1,200 respectively; those going to KarnalChouk, Sangli, or Vijaynagar face a higher fee of ₹2,000 per month. The final stop at Miraj incurs the highest monthly cost of ₹2,300. This bus route ensures that students can easily reach and leave campus, enhancing their overall commuting experience while managing costs effectively.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers its students a convenient college bus service named "Ashta-Shirala." This route serves several important locations, including Gotkhindi/Walwa Fata/Bawachi, Tuzarpur/Gatadwadi, Islampur, Pethnaka, RethareDharan, Red, and finally Shirala. The monthly transport costs vary depending on the stop: students traveling from Gotkhindi/Walwa Fata/Bawachi pay ₹1,000 per month, while those boarding at Tuzarpur/Gatadwadi face a fee of ₹1,300. As one moves further along the route, the cost increases to ₹1,600 for Islampur and ₹1,700 for Pethnaka. RethareDharan commuters pay ₹1,900, Red residents contribute ₹2,000, and those heading to Shirala are charged the highest at ₹2,200 per month. This bus service ensures that students from various locations can easily commute to the college, making their daily journey more manageable and stress-free.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers its students a convenient college bus service named "Ashta-Kolhapur," which connects them to various locations including Bgani, Shigaon, Vadgaon, Top, Shiye Phata, Shiroli, and finally Kolhapur. The route is designed to cater to the diverse residential needs of the student body, with each stop reflecting a different part of the region. Monthly transport costs vary based on the distance from Ashta; Bgani charges ₹1,200 per month, while the cost increases significantly for Shiroli and Kolhapur at ₹2,800 and ₹3,000 respectively. Students traveling to intermediate stops like Vadgaon or Top will pay ₹2,000 and ₹2,500 monthly, ensuring that the fare structure is both fair and reflective of the distance covered. This bus service not only facilitates regular commuting but also enhances the overall campus experience by providing reliable transportation options for students residing in different parts of Ashta and Kolhapur.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers its students a convenient college bus service named "Ashta-Dudhondi" for their daily commute. This route spans across several towns and villages, including Ashta, Nagthane Fata, Amnapur, Burli, Kirloskarwadi, and finally Dudhondi. The monthly transport cost varies depending on the stop: students boarding at Ashta will pay ₹500 per month, while those from more distant locations such as Amnapur and Kirloskarwadi face higher charges of ₹1,300 and ₹1,900 respectively. The route concludes in Dudhondi, where the monthly fee stands at ₹2,300. This comprehensive bus service ensures that students have a reliable and cost-effective means to travel between their homes and the college campus, facilitating their academic journey throughout the 2025-26 academic year.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers its students a convenient college bus service named "Ashta-Tasgaon" for their daily commute. This route covers several important locations including Ashta, Audumbar Fata, Bhilawadi, Khandobachiwadi/Malwadi, Bhilawadi Station, Pachva Mil, and finally Tasgaon. The monthly transport costs vary based on the stop: students traveling from Ashta pay ₹500 per month, while those from Audumbar Fata and Bhilawadi face a higher fee of ₹700 each. Khandobachiwadi/Malwadi residents must contribute ₹900 per month, followed by a significant increase to ₹1,300 for students boarding at the Bhilawadi Station. The journey concludes with a hefty monthly fare of ₹2,200 for Tasgaon commuters. This diverse pricing structure ensures that students from different residential areas can affordably access their educational institution through reliable and efficient bus services.

The Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers convenient transportation via its designated "Ashta-Karad" college bus route to facilitate student commutes. The route covers several important locations including Karad, Malkapur, Atake, Wathar, Kasegaon, and Pethnaka. Monthly transport costs vary depending on the stop: students traveling from Karad will pay ₹3,300 per month, while those from Malkapur, Atake, Wathar, or Kasegaon face a cost of ₹2,800 to ₹2,500 respectively. Pethnaka residents can expect to pay the lowest fee at ₹2,200 per month. This bus service ensures that students have reliable and affordable transportation options, enhancing their overall commuting experience while focusing on academic pursuits during the 2025-26 academic year.

For the Mechanical Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the 2025-26 academic year, the admission cutoffs for MHT-CET merit marks vary significantly across different reservation categories. The General seat has the highest cutoff, with an OPEN category requiring a minimum of 76.93 marks, closely followed by the SEBC and TFWS categories at 75.40 and 83.11 marks respectively.

For other categories, the merit mark requirements are lower but still substantial. The OBC category demands a score of 65.31 for General seats and 68.47 for Ladies seats. Similarly, the SC category requires a minimum of 40.68 marks for General seats and 36.23 for Ladies seats. The NT-1 and NT-2 categories also have notable merit mark requirements with NT-1 needing at least 71.66 marks and NT-2 requiring 71.48 marks, both for General seats.

The VJ category has a slightly lower cutoff of 50.70 marks for General seats and 44.26 for Ladies seats. The DEF category requires 52.36 marks for General seats but does not specify a mark requirement for Ladies seats. Lastly, the EWS category demands at least 28.98 marks for General seats.

It's important to note that some categories like ST and TFWS do not have specified merit marks for Ladies seats, indicating either no specific cutoff or that the data is not available for this particular category. These varied cutoffs reflect the diverse reservation policies in place and ensure a fair admission process for students from different socio-economic backgrounds.

For the AERO branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the academic year 2025-26, the MHT-CET merit marks required for various reservation categories vary significantly. The General category requires a minimum of 76.27 marks (OPEN) to secure an admission, while the VJ category has a lower threshold at 8.71 marks. For other categories, the requirements are as follows: NT-1 and NT-3 have cutoffs of 75.65 and 62.14 marks respectively; SEBC requires 74.06 marks; SC students need to score at least 54.09 marks; DEF candidates must secure a minimum of 70.50 marks, and EWS candidates require just 7.44 marks.

Ladies seats have specific cutoffs for some categories: General (8.71), NT-2 (62.86), OBC (71.82), SEBC (74.01), OPEN (79.69), and SC (36.12). Notably, there are no specified merit marks for the ST category in any of the categories, indicating that this category might not have a separate cutoff or may be merged with other categories. Additionally, NT-3 does not specify a merit mark for ladies seats.

It is crucial to note that these cutoffs represent the minimum marks required and do not guarantee admission as the final selection process also considers other factors such as availability of seats in each category. Students are advised to stay informed about any additional requirements or changes in the admission process.

For the Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26, the MHT-CET merit marks required for admission vary significantly across different reservation categories and notification types. The General category requires a minimum cutoff of 75.21 to 90.43 marks depending on the notification type, with NT-3 having the lowest at 77.01 marks and TFWS having the highest at 90.43 marks. For the Ladies category within the General seat, the required merit marks are slightly higher, ranging from 78.34 to 87.41.

For other reservation categories, the cutoffs are notably different. The OBC category demands a minimum of 84.33 to 90.87 marks across various notifications, with NT-2 having the highest at 88.56 for the General seat and SEBC requiring the highest at 90.87 in the Ladies category. The SC category has lower cutoffs ranging from 70.50 to 73.75 marks, while the ST category requires a minimum of 63.65 marks with no specific merit mark for the Ladies category.

The VJ category has a General seat cutoff of 75.21 and an OPEN category with a higher requirement of 87.14 marks. The DEF category also follows a similar pattern, requiring 81.94 marks in the General seat without a specified mark for the Ladies category. Lastly, the EWS category has a cutoff of 71.56 to 73.75 marks, with no specific merit mark mentioned for the Ladies category.

These varied cutoffs reflect the diverse reservation policies and the competitive nature of different categories in securing admission to this prestigious engineering college.

For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the AIDS branch has set specific merit mark cutoffs for various reservation categories based on MHT-CET scores. The General category requires a minimum score of 73.44 to 91.30 marks across different notification types (NT-1, NT-2, VJ, TFWS), with the highest being 91.30 for the TFWS category. For the Ladies seats in these categories, the cutoffs are slightly higher, ranging from 75.80 to 91.30 marks.

The OBC category has a general seat cutoff of 63.47 and a ladies seat cutoff of 71.82 marks. The SC category requires significantly lower scores with a general seat cutoff of 54.09 and a ladies seat cutoff of just 36.12, indicating the college's commitment to inclusive education. The ST category does not have any merit mark cutoffs specified for either general or ladies seats.

Other categories include DEF (Defence), with a general seat cutoff of 70.51 marks; EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) with a general seat cutoff of 70.62 marks, and NT-3 category which has a lower general seat cutoff of 73.44 for both genders.

It is important to note that the TFWS category has no merit mark specified for the general seats, possibly indicating a different admission process or criteria specific to this category. This detailed breakdown helps students understand their eligibility based on their MHT-CET scores and the various reservation categories available at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology.

For the RAI branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the 2025-26 academic year, the MHT-CET merit mark cutoffs vary significantly across different reservation categories. The General category requires a minimum of 79.72 marks for the OPEN seat, while VJ (Very High Income) candidates need at least 72.38 marks to secure admission in the General category. For OBC (Other Backward Classes), the cutoff is set at 74.06 marks for General seats and 75.24 marks for Ladies' seats.

The SC (Scheduled Castes) category has a lower threshold, with a minimum of 41.75 marks required for General seats, and an even lower requirement of 47.36 marks for Ladies' seats. Similarly, the ST (Scheduled Tribes) category demands at least 47.05 marks for General seats, though no data is provided for Ladies' seats in this category.

The NT-1 to NT-3 categories have varying cutoffs; NT-2 has the highest requirement of 78.27 marks for General seats and 75.24 marks for Ladies' seats. The TFWS (Tribal Sub Category - Female Welfare Scheme) category demands a minimum of 85.62 marks, with no data provided for Ladies' seats.

DEF (Defence Forces) candidates need to secure at least 18.55 marks for General seats and EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) require 45.83 marks, again with no data provided for Ladies' seats in these categories. These cutoffs reflect the diverse reservation policies and merit-based selection criteria applied by the college to ensure equitable access to higher education.

For the Civil Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the 2025-26 academic year, the MHT-CET merit mark cutoffs vary significantly across different reservation categories. The General category requires a minimum of 73.63 marks for an OPEN seat, while VJ community students need to secure at least 63.82 marks. For Other Backward Classes (OBC), the cutoff is notably higher, with both general and ladies seats requiring 66.43 and 69.89 marks respectively. Scheduled Caste (SC) candidates have a lower threshold of 46.40 for general seats and 72.10 for ladies seats. The Special Category (ST) does not have any cutoffs provided, indicating that all eligible ST students are likely to be admitted based on their merit.

The Non-Technical (NT) category includes three subcategories: NT-1 with a cutoff of 29.81, NT-2 with 71.85 for general seats and 57.40 for ladies, and NT-3 with 48.56 marks for general seats without any specific lady's seat cutoff provided. The VJ community also has a separate category with its own cutoffs.

For the reserved categories, the Forward Class (FC) students from TFWS need to secure at least 74.01 marks, while EWS candidates have a significantly lower requirement of just 12.96 marks for general seats and none for ladies seats. It's important to note that no specific cutoffs are provided for DEF and ST categories, suggesting these might be filled based on merit alone or through other admission policies.

These varied cutoffs reflect the college’s commitment to inclusive education while maintaining academic standards across different reservation categories.

For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the cutoff marks for the ELECT (Electronics and Telecommunication) branch under MHT-CET are diverse across various reservation categories. The General seat has the highest cutoff among all categories, with an average score of 73.75 marks required for NT-2, followed closely by 66.17 marks for NT-1 and 70.62 marks for OBC (Other Backward Classes). For SC (Scheduled Castes), the General seat cutoff is notably lower at 49.73 marks, while VJ (Very Jasu) category requires a minimum of 53.76 marks, which is slightly higher than the General seat.

The OPEN (General) category has the highest merit mark requirement, with both general and ladies seats demanding scores above 80 marks, specifically 77.95 for general and 81.97 for ladies. The SEBC (Specially Educated Backward Classes) category also demands a high score of 76.93 for the General seat, while EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) requires a lower cutoff at 45.36 marks.

Notably, some categories such as NT-3 and ST (Scheduled Tribes) do not have any merit mark data provided for this academic year. Additionally, DEF (Defence) category has a General seat cutoff of 54.59 marks, while TFWS (Tribal Families Welfare Scheme) requires the highest score among all categories at 82.97 marks.

Ladies seats generally show higher cutoffs compared to general seats across most categories, with notable exceptions like SC and EWS where ladies' scores are lower than their general counterparts. This pattern reflects the broader reservation policies aimed at providing equal opportunities for women in engineering education.

For the Food branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the 2025-26 academic year, the MHT-CET merit marks required for admission vary significantly across different reservation categories. The General seat cutoff ranges from a minimum of 59.10 marks (EWS) to a maximum of 89.54 marks (OPEN). For the OBC category, the general seat cutoff is set at 86.34 marks, while for SEBC and OPEN seats, it is notably higher at 88.98 and 89.54 marks respectively. The SC reservation requires a minimum of 75.10 marks to secure a general seat, whereas the EWS category has a lower cutoff of 59.10 marks.

Notably, there are no specific merit mark requirements mentioned for the VJ, NT-1, and ST categories. However, for the OBC, SEBC, OPEN, SC, and EWS reservations, both general and ladies seats have distinct cutoffs, with the ladies seat cutoff often being higher than the general seat cutoff. For instance, in the SC category, the ladies seat requires a minimum of 83.26 marks to secure admission, compared to 75.10 for the general seat.

The NT-2 reservation stands out with a significant merit mark requirement of 87.90 for the general seat, which is notably higher than other categories. It's important to note that for some categories like NT-1, TFWS, and DEF, there are no specific merit marks provided in this data set.

Overall, securing admission into the Food branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology will depend on achieving a high enough MHT-CET merit mark within one's reservation category. Students from OBC, SEBC, and EWS categories have higher chances due to their respective cutoffs being among the highest.

For the CSE IOT branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the academic year 2025-26, the MHT-CET merit mark cutoffs vary significantly across different reservation categories. The General category requires a minimum of 83.07 marks for an OPEN seat, while VJ (Very Poorly Backward) candidates need at least 69.22 marks to secure admission in a general seat. For OBC (Other Backward Classes), the cutoff is set higher at 79.70 and 81.01 marks for general and ladies seats respectively, indicating a strong emphasis on merit. SC (Scheduled Castes) candidates have a lower cutoff of 64.71 and 68.47 marks for general and ladies seats, respectively.

The NT-2 category has the highest cutoff at 81.80 and 79.30 marks for general and ladies seats, while SEBC (Specially Educated Backward Classes) and TFWS (Tribal Welfare Scheme) candidates need to secure a minimum of 82.97 and 83.88 marks in the general category, respectively. The DEF (Defence) category has a cutoff of 71.17 for general seats but no data is available for ladies seats.

Notably, there are no merit mark cutoffs provided for ST (Scheduled Tribes), EWS (Economically Weaker Sections), and NT-1 categories in the general seat category. This suggests that these categories might have specific eligibility criteria or a different admission process. For ladies seats, the cutoffs vary widely across categories; however, the highest is observed for SEBC at 84.64 marks.

In summary, securing an admission to the CSE IOT branch requires students to meet stringent merit mark requirements that differ based on their reservation category and gender.

For the academic year 2025-26, Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has specific criteria for 12th-standard students seeking admission. The college accepts candidates who have completed their 12th grade with either Open (Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics) or (Physics, Mathematics + Any Technical Subject), requiring a minimum of 134 out of 135 marks. For candidates belonging to the Cast category, the requirement is slightly lower, necessitating a minimum of 119 out of 120 marks in their 12th-grade examination with Physics, Chemistry, and Mathematics or Physics, Mathematics, and any Technical Subject. These criteria ensure that students from diverse backgrounds meet the academic standards set by the institution for engineering programs.

Over the past three academic years, Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has demonstrated steady growth in its overall placement performance. In the 2023-24 academic year, a total of 578 students secured placements, with 554 offers being extended to them. This number increased slightly in the following year, with 620 students receiving 511 job offers during the 2024-25 academic period. For the most recent 2025-26 academic year, while specific numbers of students and offers are not disclosed, the college managed to visit a significant number of companies—105 in total, marking an increase from the previous years' visits.

The trend indicates that the college has been successful in attracting more employers each year. The 90+ companies visited in 2024-25 and the 105 companies visited in 2025-26 suggest a growing interest from industry leaders to engage with students, potentially leading to better placement opportunities for future batches. Despite not disclosing average salaries or highest package figures, these trends point towards an improving employment landscape for graduates of Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology. The consistent growth in the number of companies visiting the campus each year is a testament to the college's reputation and its ability to foster strong industry connections.

Over the past three academic years at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the Mechanical Engineering branch has demonstrated a steady improvement in its placement outcomes. In the 2023-24 academic year, 112 students secured job offers with an average package of 4.5 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), and the highest package was recorded at 33 LPA. The following year, 2024-25 saw a slight decrease in the number of students to 87, but this was more than offset by a significant increase in offers to 132. This period also witnessed an improvement in average packages, with the mean salary rising to 4.7 LPA, and the highest package reached an impressive 6.5 LPA.

The 2025-26 academic year further solidified the branch's strong placement record, with 96 students securing job offers. Despite a modest decrease in the number of placements compared to the previous year, the average package increased to 5 LPA, and the highest package achieved was an outstanding 8.5 LPA. These trends indicate a consistent upward trajectory in both the quantity and quality of placement opportunities for Mechanical Engineering graduates from Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology over these three years. The increasing average packages and highest packages suggest that employers are recognizing the value of the college's Mechanical Engineering program, offering better compensation to its students.

Over the past three academic years, the Civil Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has demonstrated a steady improvement in its placement outcomes. In the 2023-24 academic year, 73 students secured job offers with an average package of 99 lakh per annum (LPA) and a highest package reaching 6 LPA. This marks a strong start for the branch.

The following year, 2024-25 saw a slight decrease in the number of students to 70, but there was a notable increase in the average placement offer to 4.8 LPA, with the highest package jumping significantly to 7.5 LPA. This improvement suggests that companies are increasingly recognizing and rewarding the skills and knowledge imparted by the Civil Engineering program.

In the most recent academic year, 2025-26, the number of students decreased further to 34, but there was a marked increase in the average package to 5 LPA. The highest package achieved that year was 6.5 LPA, indicating continued competitiveness and demand for graduates from this branch.

Overall, these trends suggest an upward trajectory in placement outcomes for Civil Engineering students at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology, with a particular emphasis on higher average packages over the last two years. This performance reflects both the quality of education provided by the college and the growing recognition of civil engineering skills in the job market.

Over the past three academic years, the Electrical Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has demonstrated a robust placement record. In 2023-24, with 145 students graduating, 117 received job offers, averaging an annual package of 4.8 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), with the highest package reaching up to 9 LPA. The following year, 2024-25 saw a slight increase in both student numbers and placements; out of 146 graduates, 127 found employment, marking an improvement in average packages to 5.2 LPA, while the top package remained at 9 LPA.

The most significant leap occurred in 2025-26, where the number of students increased to 153 and so did the placements, with 171 offers being made. This year also saw an improvement in average packages, rising to 5 LPA, though the highest package dropped slightly to 6.5 LPA. These trends indicate a positive trajectory for the placement outcomes in the Electrical Engineering branch, with both the number of job offers and the average salary increasing over the three-year period, despite some volatility in the highest package achieved.

Over the past three academic years, the Computer Science Engineering (CSE) branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has demonstrated a steady improvement in placement outcomes for its students. In the 2023-24 academic year, 153 CSE students secured job offers, with an average package of 5 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum) and a highest package reaching up to 10 LPA. This initial year set a solid foundation for future performance.

The following year, 2024-25 saw a slight decrease in the number of students participating in placements—down to 131—but there was an increase in the average package to 5.2 LPA, with the highest package being offered at 8 LPA. This trend suggests that while the overall number of participants decreased, the quality and competitiveness of the job offers improved.

In the most recent academic year, 2025-26, the CSE branch experienced a modest increase in both student numbers (145) and placement offers (120). The average package remained consistent at 5.2 LPA, but the highest package achieved a significant leap to 12.34 LPA. This improvement indicates that the college's efforts in enhancing its curriculum and industry connections have positively impacted students' employability.

Overall, these trends suggest a growing trend in the placement performance of CSE graduates from Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology, with notable improvements seen in both average and highest package values over the three-year period.

Over the past three years, the Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has shown a steady growth in both student numbers and placement outcomes. In 2023-24, there were no students placed with no offers reported, leaving average and highest package details undisclosed. The following year, 2024-25, saw an improvement with 62 students securing placements out of which 40 received job offers. The average salary for these placements was 5.2 Lakh Per Annum (LPA), while the highest package reached 7.5 LPA.

In the subsequent year, 2025-26, there was a slight increase in both student numbers and placement offers with 69 students placed and 41 offers issued. Notably, the average salary remained consistent at 5.2 LPA, but the highest package saw a significant jump to 8.67 LPA. This indicates that while the overall average did not change significantly from the previous year, there was an improvement in the top-end of the salary distribution, suggesting a more competitive placement scenario with higher-paying opportunities for select students.

source_section: placement_3yr_summary/Artifical Inteligence and Data Science Engg
text:
Over the past three academic years, the Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering (AI & DS) branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has shown a steady growth in its placement performance. In 2023-24, despite having no students securing placements, there were also no job offers made to graduates from this branch. This period set the stage for improvement in subsequent years.

By 2024-25, the AI & DS branch saw significant progress with 70 students participating and receiving a total of 47 job offers. The average package for these placements was recorded at 5.2 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), though the highest package was not disclosed. This year marked an encouraging start to the placement journey for the AI & DS branch.

In the following academic year, 2025-26, there was a slight increase in both the number of students and job offers. With 73 students participating, only 43 placements were secured, reflecting a decrease from the previous year's numbers. Despite this, the average package remained consistent at 5.2 LPA, while the highest package achieved was notably higher at 7.5 LPA. This trend suggests that while there may have been fewer offers overall, those who did secure positions received significantly better remuneration.

Overall, the AI & DS branch has demonstrated a promising trajectory in terms of placement outcomes, with an increase in both student participation and average package value over the three-year period. The highest package achieved in 2025-26 stands out as a notable highlight, indicating that despite fluctuations in the number of placements secured, the quality of job offers has been on an upward trend.

text:
Over the past three academic years, the Aeronautical Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has shown a steady trend in its placement outcomes. In the 2023-24 academic year, 55 students secured job offers with an average package of 4.3 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), and the highest package was recorded at 5.5 LPA. This year saw a slight reduction in both the number of students and placements; in 2024-25, 33 out of 38 students received job offers, resulting in an average package of 4.6 LPA, with the highest being 4.5 LPA. The following academic year, 2025-26, witnessed a modest increase in both student numbers and placements to 48 and 33 respectively. Despite this, the average package remained consistent at 4.6 LPA, while the highest package soared to an impressive 6 LPA.

These trends indicate that while there is some variability year-to-year, the Aeronautical Engineering branch consistently provides competitive placement opportunities for its students. The highest package of 6 LPA in 2025-26 stands out as a significant achievement and reflects the strong demand for talent in this field from industry players. Overall, these results underscore the college's commitment to preparing students for successful careers in the aeronautical sector.

Over the past three academic years, the Food Technology branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta has demonstrated a robust placement record. In 2023-24, with an intake of 40 students, the college managed to secure placements for 90% of them, offering an average package of ₹3.8 lakh per annum (LPA), with the highest package reaching up to ₹4.5 LPA.

The following year, 2024-25, saw a reduction in student intake to 21, but despite this decrease, the placement rate remained strong at 81%, with an average package of ₹3.5 LPA and the top package being ₹4 LPA. This suggests that while the number of students decreased, the quality or demand for placements did not diminish significantly.

In 2025-26, the college saw a further reduction in intake to just 8 students, yet it managed an impressive placement rate of 263%, with each student securing multiple offers. The average package improved slightly to ₹4 LPA, and the highest package achieved was ₹5 LPA. This year's performance indicates that despite a smaller cohort, the college maintained high standards in placing its students.

Overall, these trends suggest an improving placement scenario for Food Technology graduates from this institution over the past three years, with both average and highest packages showing positive movements. The higher placement rate and increased number of offers per student in 2025-26 highlight the growing demand for skilled professionals in the field of food technology.

The campus recruitment drive at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta during the academic year 2022-23 was a robust affair with 69 companies visiting to recruit students. The IT sector dominated with significant participation from leading firms such as TCS, which had a presence across all its locations; Atos, Tech Mahindra, Deloitte, Capgemini, and others like Sankey Solutions, Aloha Technology, and DeltaX. These IT giants were joined by core/manufacturing companies including SiriusFoods, KPIT, Wipro-Pari, Bharat Forge, Sunbeam, ZF India, Vibha India Tooling, Faurecia, PGS Techno, Right Health Ltd, Indovance, Paras Aerospace, National Payments Corporation of India, Marker Strive, SCG Design, Karad Projects, Manikaran Power Ltd, and Deccan Infra Pvt Ltd. The mix of IT companies (40) was notably higher than the core/industrial sector participants (29), reflecting a strong demand for tech talent in the current job market.

The variety of branches eligible for placements was extensive, catering to diverse engineering disciplines. Students from all streams including Computer Science and Engineering, Electronics and Telecommunication, Mechanical, Civil, and Electrical Engineering were able to apply for roles that aligned with their specializations. The presence of companies like TCS and Capgemini not only offered a wide range of IT opportunities but also provided insights into the latest technologies and industry trends. Similarly, core sector companies such as Bharat Forge and ZF India presented prospects in manufacturing and automotive industries, ensuring a balanced mix of opportunities for students across different engineering branches.

This year's campus recruitment drive at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta saw a robust participation from 72 companies, marking a significant engagement with both the tech-savvy and manufacturing sectors. The IT sector was well-represented by prominent names such as KPIT, DeltaX, Tech Mahindra, Hexaware, Rheal Software, Sahaj Soft, Numetry Technologies, ACME Infovision, Amazon, Accenture, Sankey Solution, Rsquare Software, Beacon, Globalion, TCS, DemandFarm, Persistent, Exa Mobility, Micron, and Zensar. These companies offered a wide array of opportunities in software development, data analytics, cloud computing, and more.

On the other hand, core/manufacturing companies like Neologic, Klassic Wheels LTD, Technip Energies, RDC Concrete, Worley, DC Drones, Delval, Magic Stone LLP, Technimont, GestAmp, Wipro-Pari, Maker Strive, Fre-Voltgreen, Bharat Forge, Spped Global, KSB Pumps, Forvia-Faurecia, BVG Group, and Adani also participated. This mix of IT and core industries provided a diverse range of roles for students from various branches including Computer Science Engineering (CSE), Information Technology (IT), Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering (E&TC), Mechanical Engineering, Civil Engineering, and more.

The first 40 companies listed included a blend of these sectors, ensuring that students across different engineering disciplines had ample opportunities to explore their career interests. The drive not only highlighted the growing demand for tech professionals but also underscored the increasing importance of core engineering skills in today's industrial landscape. With such a diverse mix of companies and roles, the placement year 2023-24 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology promised exciting opportunities for its students.

This year's campus recruitment drive at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta saw an impressive 82 companies visit the campus, marking a significant increase in participation compared to previous years. The event was well-received by students across various engineering disciplines. Among the IT sector participants were prominent names such as ACME Infovision, Hexaware, Neutrino Tech Systems, Accenture, Softenger, Rsquare Soft., Thoughtworks, Tech Mahindra, Rheal Soft, Test Yantra, Hati International, Weverve, Quality Kiosk, Cognizant, TCS, and Infosys (female-only), reflecting the growing importance of software and IT services in the job market. Notable core/manufacturing companies included Atlas Copco, IPAC Automation, Microverse Automation, Kirdat Group, Neilsoft, Schinder Electric, Fox Solution, Free Volt, Thomas & Brian, Technip Ener., Karamtara Engineering Pvt Ltd, Cooper, Infra Market, FleetGuared, Avaada Group, Gestamp, PPS, S J Contracts Pvt Ltd, Adani, and S S Pathane Aoss. Pvt Ltd Pune. The mix of IT companies (42) to core industries (38) was relatively balanced, providing a diverse range of opportunities for students from all branches including mechanical, electrical, civil, and computer science engineering. This variety ensured that students could explore roles in both software development and hardware manufacturing, catering to their specific interests and career aspirations.

companies: S S Pathane Aoss. Pvt Ltd Pune

text:
The academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta witnessed a robust and diverse placement season across various engineering branches. The college reported an impressive total of 588 job offers till date, with 105 companies visiting the campus to recruit students.

Mechanical Engineering stood out with 96 students securing placements, receiving 122 job offers on average paying around 5 LPA, with a top package of 8.5 LPA. This branch demonstrated strong demand from industries looking for skilled professionals in mechanical engineering roles.

Civil Engineering had 34 students placed, with 37 offers averaging 5 LPA and the highest package being 6.5 LPA. The placement numbers were encouraging, reflecting a steady market for civil engineers.

Electrical Engineering saw an impressive performance with 153 students securing placements through 171 job offers at an average of 5 LPA, with the highest package reaching 6.5 LPA. This branch's success was attributed to its relevance in today’s technology-driven industries.

Computer Science Engineering emerged as a high-performing branch, with 145 students receiving 120 job offers averaging 5.2 LPA and the top package of 12.34 LPA. The strong performance highlighted the immense demand for skilled software professionals in various sectors.

The newly introduced Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering branch showcased a promising start, with 69 students securing 41 placements at an average salary of 5.2 LPA and a top package of 8.67 LPA. This new addition to the curriculum was well-received by both students and industry.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering had 73 students placed through 43 job offers, averaging 5.2 LPA with a highest package of 7.5 LPA. The growing importance of AI and data science in various industries was evident from the high demand for talent in this field.

Aeronautical Engineering saw 48 students receive placements with 33 job offers at an average salary of 4.6 LPA, with the top package being 6 LPA. This branch's performance reflected a balanced market and strong industry interest.

Food Technology had the smallest but notable group of 8 students who secured 21 job offers, averaging 4 LPA with the highest package of 5 LPA. The success in this niche field highlighted the diverse opportunities available to students across different engineering disciplines.

Overall, the placement season at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology was marked by a high level of engagement from both students and industry partners. The college's commitment to providing quality education and fostering strong industry connections played a crucial role in ensuring that each branch achieved its best possible outcomes.

branches: Artifical Inteligence and Data Science Engg

text:
The 2024-25 academic year at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta saw a robust and diverse placement season across various engineering branches. Mechanical Engineering stood out with an impressive 132 job offers for its 87 students, averaging 4.7 LPA, with the highest package reaching up to 6.5 LPA. This branch demonstrated strong demand from employers, reflecting the practical skills and technical knowledge valued in the industry.

Civil Engineering also performed well, securing 30 placements out of 70 students, offering an average salary of 4.8 LPA, with a top package of 7.5 LPA. The Civil branch's placement success highlights the growing importance of infrastructure development and construction projects.

Electrical Engineering showcased its prowess by attracting 127 job offers for its 146 students, averaging 5.2 LPA, and boasting a highest package of 9 LPA. This high average reflects the strong demand for electrical engineers in both traditional and emerging sectors like renewable energy and smart grids.

Computer Science Engineering had 90 placements out of 131 applicants, with an average salary of 5.2 LPA and a top package of 8 LPA. The branch's performance underscores the high value placed on software development, cybersecurity, and data science skills in today’s tech-driven job market.

The newly introduced Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering saw 40 placements out of 62 students, averaging 5.2 LPA with a top package of 7.5 LPA. This branch's success indicates the increasing importance of Internet of Things (IoT) and cybersecurity in various industries.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering also performed commendably, offering 47 placements to its 70 students, averaging 5.2 LPA with a top package of 6 LPA. The strong performance here reflects the growing demand for professionals skilled in AI and data analytics.

Aeronautical Engineering had a more modest success with 28 placements out of 33 applicants, averaging 4.6 LPA, with the highest package at 4.5 LPA. This branch's placement numbers suggest that while there is significant interest from employers, the field remains competitive.

Food Technology offered 17 placements to its 21 students, averaging 3.5 LPA, and topping out at 4 LPA. The lower average reflects the more specialized nature of this field, which may have limited demand compared to other branches but still provides valuable job opportunities for graduates.

Overall, Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology visited by 90 companies managed to secure a total of 511 placements (as of the date provided) across all branches. This comprehensive placement drive reflects the college's commitment to providing students with industry-relevant skills and knowledge, ensuring they are well-prepared for their future careers. The diverse range of packages offered—from as low as 3.5 LPA to a high of 9 LPA—demonstrates the varied opportunities available in today’s job market, catering to different career aspirations and skill sets.

branches: Artifical Inteligence and Data Science Engg

text:
The 2023-24 academic year at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta witnessed a robust placement season with a total of 554 offers across various engineering branches. The college hosted visits from 82 companies, creating an energetic and competitive environment among students.

In the Mechanical Engineering department, 112 students secured job offers averaging at 4.5 LPA, with the highest package reaching up to 33 LPA. This branch demonstrated strong performance, reflecting a high demand for mechanical engineers in the industry.

Civil Engineering graduates fared well too, with 73 students receiving 99 offers, maintaining an average salary of 4.5 LPA and achieving the highest package at 6 LPA. The department's success underscores the relevance of civil engineering skills in today’s market.

Electrical Engineering emerged as a high-performing branch, boasting 145 students who received 117 job offers with an impressive average salary of 4.8 LPA and a top package of 9 LPA. This strong performance indicates a significant demand for electrical engineers.

Computer Science Engineering showed robust results, with 153 students securing 142 job offers at an average salary of 5 LPA and the highest package reaching up to 10 LPA. The high number of placements in this branch reflects the growing importance of technology in various industries.

Notably, the Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering and Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering branches did not have any students or job offers recorded for the academic year. This absence could be due to a smaller student body or recent establishment of these programs, making it challenging to gauge their placement outcomes yet.

Aeronautical Engineering had 55 students who received 55 job offers with an average salary of 4.3 LPA and a top package of 5.5 LPA. This branch's performance was commendable, despite the relatively smaller number of participants.

Food Technology saw 40 students receive 36 job offers at an average salary of 3.8 LPA, with the highest being 4.5 LPA. The department’s success in placing a significant portion of its student body highlights the growing interest and demand for food technology professionals.

Overall, the placement season was marked by a high level of engagement from both students and companies, resulting in a diverse range of job offers across all branches. This comprehensive performance reflects the college's commitment to providing quality education and preparing students for successful careers.

branches: Artifical Inteligence and Data Science Engg

text: For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26, students applying for B.Tech., BBA, or BCA programs are required to submit a comprehensive set of documents. These include the MHT-CET or JEE 2026 Marksheet, along with their 10th and 12th standard mark sheets, as well as any relevant diploma mark sheet if applicable. Additionally, students must provide a Leaving or Transfer Certificate, an AADHAR Card (ensuring it is linked to a mobile number), a Migration Certificate, Caste Certificate, and the Caste/Tribe Validity Certificate. For financial assistance, an Income Certificate for 2025-26 is necessary. Students from specific categories must also submit additional documents: Orphan candidates need to provide proof of orphanage status, while those with disabilities should present a Disability certificate issued by the District Civil Surgeon. The Non-Creamy Layer Certificate (valid until 31 March 2027) and EWS Certificate are required for socio-economically disadvantaged students. For minority categories, a Minority Certificate is mandatory, and students from specific regions may need to provide an Alpabudharka (Tahsildar) document. These requirements ensure that the college can accurately assess eligibility for admissions and scholarships, catering to diverse student backgrounds.

documents: Non - Creamy layer Certificate (valid upto 31 March 2027)
Disability certificate (Online) from District Civil Surgeon

text: For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in the academic year 2025-26 under the DSY category, students are required to submit a comprehensive set of documents. These include essential academic records such as the 10th Marksheet, 12th Marksheet, and Diploma Marksheet, which provide evidence of educational qualifications. Additionally, a Leaving / Transfer Certificate is needed to confirm the student's previous institution details. For identification purposes, an AADHAR Card (with linked mobile number) must be provided. Caste-specific documents like Caste Certificate and Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate are mandatory for confirming the student’s caste category eligibility. The Non-Creamy Layer Certificate is valid until 31 March 2027, ensuring that students from non-privileged backgrounds can apply for scholarships. An Income Certificate for the year 2025-26 is required to assess financial status. For special categories, an Orphan candidate certificate and a Disability certificate (Online) from the District Civil Surgeon are necessary. Students from minority communities must submit a Minority Certificate. The EWS Certificate further caters to economically weaker sections of society. Notably, caste-category-specific requirements include valid Caste / Tribe Validity Certificates and Non-Creamy Layer Certificates, ensuring that all eligible students can apply for available scholarships and admission opportunities.
